coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of morin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Yanan
Li, Lin
Zhang, Huilin
Du, Fangfang
Meng, Yating
Shuang, Shaomin
Wang, Ruibing
Song, Shengmei
Dong, Chuan
coredata.dc:description: The B,N dual-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescence detection the morin were prepared from sodium tetraborate and polyethyleneimine through the single-step hydrothermal method. The B,N-CDs exhibited the optimum excitation and emission wavelength at 340 nm and 467 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the intensities of emission peak at 467 nm of B,N-CDs reduced meanwhile a new peak emerged at 560 nm with the continuous addition of morin, which revealed the ratio fluorescence characteristic between F560nm/F467nm and morin concentration with the linearity range and detection limit of 14.5–32.5 μmol/L and 0.3 μmol/L (S/N = 3), respectively. The interference of common antibiotics and remedies could be ignored when the concentration of morin was detected by the B,N-CDs, which demonstrating the outstanding selectivity. Furthermore, the proposed fluorescence method is used to detect morin in urine with recoveries are 99.8–104.5%. The results of this research indicate the feasibility and practicality of B,N-CDs as an effective fluorescent probe for the determination of morin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B,N-CDs
Ratio fluorescence
Morin
Determination

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of quencher time, pH and temperature on the reaction
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of morin
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of B,N-CDs by morin
Detection of morin in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy image (top right inset). (b) Diameter distribution of B,N-CDs.
(a) XPS survey, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) B1s XPS and (e) FTIR spectra of B,N-CDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra and PL spectra of B,N-CDs (5 mg/mL). The inset is images of the B,N-CD aqueous solution in sunlight (left) and under UV lamp (right). (b) λex-dependent FL behavior of B,N-CDs.
(a) Effect of F0/F by different concentrations of NaCl. (b) Photostability of B,N-CDs excited at 340 nm during 80 min. (c) The influence of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs.

(a) The selectivity analysis of B,N-CDs. Comparison of fluorescence intensity of 5 mg/mL B,N-CDs after the addition of 100 μL 0.01 mol/L other different antibiotics and drugs. (b) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of 5 mg/mL B,N-CDs after the addition of 100 μL 0.01 mol/L other different antibiotics and drugs and 10 μL 0.01 mol/L morin. (c) The fluorescence spectra of 5 mg/mL B,N-CDs in the presence of morin. The concentrations of morin are: 0–191 μmol/L, respectively. The concentration of morin (1–37) are: 0, 0.47, 1.8, 2.7, 3.6, 4.5, 5.76, 7.48, 9, 10, 15, 25, 28, 32.3, 34.6, 38.5, 42.3, 46.9, 50.6, 66.3, 78.4, 84.6, 98.6, 106, 110, 118, 123, 127, 135.7, 148.3, 157.4, 164.3, 175.9, 183.5 and191 μmol/L, respectively. The inset is images of the B,N-CD (left) and B,N-CDs/morin (right) aqueous solution under UV lamp, respectively. (d) Dependence of F560 nm/F467 nm on the various concentrations of morin (14.5–32.5 μmol/L).

(a) Lifetimes of B,N-CDs (5 mg/mL) and B,N-CDs/morin (30 μmol/L). (b) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of morin (0.01 mol/L), B,N-CDs (5 mg/mL) and B,N-CDs/morin (20 μmol/L). (c) The absorbance spectra titration of morin at 5 mg/mL B,N-CDs in water. The concentrations of morin are: 14.5–32.5 μmol/L, respectively.
Abridged general view of the as-prepared B,N-CDs and sensing for morin.
Comparison of CDs prepared by different methods for the detection of morin.
Determination of morin in urine samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots for ratiometric fluorescence detection of morin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan 030006, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, and Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine
and Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Quality Research in Chinese Medicine, and Institute of Chinese Medical Sciences, University of Macau, Taipa, Macau 999078, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The B,N dual-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescence detection the morin were prepared from sodium tetraborate and polyethyleneimine through the single-step hydrothermal method. The B,N-CDs exhibited the optimum excitation and emission wavelength at 340 nm and 467 nm, respectively. Interestingly, the intensities of emission peak at 467 nm of B,N-CDs reduced meanwhile a new peak emerged at 560 nm with the continuous addition of morin, which revealed the ratio fluorescence characteristic between F560nm/F467nm and morin concentration with the linearity range and detection limit of 14.5–32.5 μmol/L and 0.3 μmol/L (S/N = 3), respectively. The interference of common antibiotics and remedies could be ignored when the concentration of morin was detected by the B,N-CDs, which demonstrating the outstanding selectivity. Furthermore, the proposed fluorescence method is used to detect morin in urine with recoveries are 99.8–104.5%. The results of this research indicate the feasibility and practicality of B,N-CDs as an effective fluorescent probe for the determination of morin.

ce:section.ce:para: In polyphenolic compounds, flavonoid play an foremost role [1]. Flavonoid are generally was viewed as potential therapeutic drugs due to it possessing many special effects. Morin is one of naturally flavonoid compounds and is found in vegetables, fruits as well as in many Chinese herbs [2], which display a host of health-promoting inwardness, such as anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, anti-diabetic, anticancer and antibacterial activity etc [3]. Morin have been suggested as dietary factors that can improve vascular function in diabetes [4]. Consequently, the detection of morin is of great importance and significance in monitoring the health of humans. To date, numerous analytical approaches have been developed to determine morin including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5,6], voltammetry [7–9] and nuclear magnetic spectroscopy (NMR) [10]. Desirable functions can be achieved at the expense of exorbitant implements and intricate pretreatment. Thence, it is highly imperative to construct an oversimplified method towards the assay of morin.

Currently, fluorescence (FL) approach has drew tremendous focus owing to its superb selectivity [11], fast response [12] and superior sensitivity [13]. Nanoparticles have a variety of unique spectroscopic, electronic, and chemical properties which have switched on a nenoteric path for fluorometry [14]. Carbon dots (CDs), as an rising star of fluorescent probes have a host of merits for instance excellent optical properties [15], excellent bio-compatibility [16], low toxicity [17]. Li et al. first constructed a based-on carbon dot sensing system for the sensitive detection of morin in a dual readout assay [18]. Tan et al. fabricated N,S-CDs to improve the sensitivity of morin detection [19]. However, the CDs were in response to morin depending on the change in fluorescence intensity under a single wavelength, which can suffer numerous factors, including surrounding environment, the probe concentration [20,21]. Ratiometric fluorescence detection can effectively overcome these factors [22,23]. Therefore, exploiting ratiometric probe of CDs towards morin is of highly important issue for reliable detection purpose.

In this paper, we constructed a ratiometric assay for the label-free detection of morin in view of B,N-CDs. The as-synthesized CDs were obtained through single-step hydrothermal route through adopting sodium tetraborate and polyethyleneimine as the precursors (Scheme 1 ). The B,N-CDs exhibited ratiometric fluorescence variation (F560nm/F467nm) with the introduction of morin under 340 nm (S/N = 3). Low limit of detection of 0.3 μmol/L was complied to detect morin. In addition, the as-constructed B,N-CDs was successfully used for the detection of morin in urine which provided a new insight into detecting morin.

Sodium tetraborate was purchased from Hebei Baoding Chemical Reagent Factory. Polyethyleneimine, sodium chloride, quinine sulfate, different common antibiotics and drugs (Cefradine (CED), Cefotaxime (CTX), Oleanolic (Ole), Lincomycin (Lin), Amoxicillin (AMX), Ursolic (Urs), Thiamphenicol (Thi), Kanamycin (KAN), Vancomycin (Van), Azithromycin (AZM), Trimethoprim (Tri), Penicillin (PEN), Tetracycline (TCY), Quercetin, Rutin, Catechin, Curcumin (Cur))and Morin were obtained from Aladdin Reagent Company.

The B,N-CDs were obtained via a single-step hydrothermal treatment. In the typical synthetic route, 0.1 g of sodium tetraborate was dissolved in 20 mL deionized water and 2 mL polyethyleneimine was added. After that, the mixture was diverted to a 50 mL matched Teflon autoclave and heated up to 200 °C for 8 h. After cooling down, through centrifugation (8000 rpm, 20 min) to remove large impurities, subsequently dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (500 Da) for 8 h. Finally, the B,N-CDs powder was collected for use.
The characterization and the involved sample preparation of prepared B,N-CDs refer to our previous articles [24–28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of quencher time, pH and temperature on the reaction
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the effect of the time when morin adding in the system, 1 mL of secondary water, 1 mL of B,N-CDs solution and 100 μL 0.01 mol/Lmorin solution were added to the cuvette. The fluorescence spectrum is measured at regular intervals.

In order to the explore the effect of the temperature on the reaction system, 1 mL of secondary water, 1 mL of B,N-CDs solution and different volume of morin solution were added to the cuvette. The concentration is 20 μmol/L, 25 μmol/L, 30 μmol/L, 35 μmol/L, 40 μmol/L, 45 μmol/L, respectively. The temperature dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior of the B,N-CDs/morin system were investigated at the temperature of 10 °C, 30 °C and 60 °C.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence performance of B,N-CDs in the presence and absence of morin are further researched. The fluorescence intensity of 1 mL buffer in the range of 1–13 with 1 mL of B,N-CDs (5 mg/mL) in 1 × 1 cm cuvette was measured, respectively. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensities were recorded when adding the morin in the pH range of 1–13. By comparing the fluorescence intensity values, the effect of pH on the B,N-CDs system is determined.

Typically, 1 mL of B,N-CDs solution was injected into quartz cuvette with a pipette simultaneously the concentration of B,N-CDs was diluted to 5 mg/mL with deionized water. Then, different concentrations of morin were separately introduced the B,N-CDs solution. To assess the selectivity of B,N-CDs, a series of common antibiotics and drugs (0.1 mol/L) were added instead of morin with the same detection condition as mentioned above. In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the established method, the fluorescence intensity of 1 mL deionized water with 1 mL B,N-CDs (5 mg/mL) in 1 × 1 cm cuvette was measured. Then 100 μL interference compounds (0.01 mol/L) were added into the system and the fluorescence intensity was measured again. Finally 10 μL morin (0.01 mol/L) was added and the fluorescence intensity was measured. The anti-interference ability was evaluated by comparing the fluorescence intensity values.
The above experiments were measured at room temperature, and all measurements were repeated 3 times.

The feasibility of this method was proved by detecting the presence of morin in urine samples. Urine samples were obtained from volunteers. The urine samples were filtered through micropore membrane, then stored at 4 °C until further analysis. In actual operation, 1 mL of the sample solution after pretreatment was adjuncted into a quartz cuvette containing 1 mL of B,N-CDs aqueous solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured. Then different concentrations of the morin solution were added to the system, measuring its fluorescence intensity. The accuracy of the method was evaluated through calculating recovery rate.

The morphology and size of the B,N-CDs were explored through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the B,N-CDs were spherical or quasi-spherical, with relatively uniform dispersion and no obvious agglomeration(Fig. 1a, insert). A relevant histogram which was acquired by statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles using the Image J software was displayed in Fig. 1b. They have a narrow range distribution from 0.5 nm to 3.0 nm. The Gaussian fitting curves show that B,N-CDs possess a average diameter of 1.8 nm.

To further inspect the surface states and molecular formula of B,N-CDs, the element analysis, XPS and FTIR were carried out. As bewrited in Table S1A, the as-prepared B,N-CDs was constituted of C 32.91, H 7.82, O 18.40, N 18.07 and B (calculated) 22.80 wt%, which confirmed the doping of N and B into CDs. Table S2B lists the relative atomic number in the light of the element content. The molecular formula for B,N-CDs is C2H7NOB2. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, the total XPS spectrum of B,N-CDs is divided into four peaks at 190.9 eV, 284.8 eV, 398.4 eV and 530.8 eV, which corresponds to B1s, C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively [29]. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of B,N-CDs in Fig. 2b presented four typical peaks at 284.6 eV (C-C),285.2 eV (C=C),286.2 eV (C-N) and 288.0 eV (C-O) [30]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the N1s spectrum demonstrated two peaks of C-N and C-NH, which situated at 399.5 eV and 401.5 eV, respectively [31]. As bewrited in Fig. 2d, the peaks at 197.16 eV, 198.10 eV and 199.80 eV were agreement with B-N, B-C, and B-O, respectively. In Fig. 2e, the band stretching of B-N and B-C was displayed at 1390 cm−1 and 1100 cm−1, respectively. The weak peak at 1643 cm−1 could be owing to C-O and C=O bonding and the typical absorption at 3420–3500 cm−1 were assigned to B-NH and B-OH bond. All results indicated that B and N have been successfully adulterated into the prepared B,N-CDs [32–34].

The fluorescence properties of B,N-CDs were characterized by fluorescence and UV–Vis spectra. In UV–Vis absorption spectra, B,N-CDs exhibited characteristic absorption peak at 350 nm (blue line in Fig. 3 a) was owing to n → π* transitions of C=O bond. Under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light, the B,N-CDs aqueous solution can show bright-blue photo-luminescence (PL) (Fig. 3a inset). The FL spectra exhibited the optimum excitation and emission wavelength at 340 nm (black line in Fig. 3a) and 467 nm (red line in Fig. 3a), respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3b, the emission wavelength of the B,N-CDs shows nearly no shift when the excitation wavelength is changed from 300 nm to 390 nm. Similar to most CDs in prior works, the B,N-CDs behaved an excitation-independent feature.

Just like pictured in Table S2 and Fig. S1, the relative fluorescence quantum yield of the B,N-CDs was calculated to be 11.17% with quinine sulfate as the reference. As shown in Fig. 4 a, even in an environment of high ionic strength, B,N-CD still shows good salt resistance. In addition, B,N-CDs was proved to have a good resistance to photobleaching in Fig. 4b. The fluorescence intensity changes in different pH buffers are shown in Fig. 4c. In the range of pH = 1–13, the fluorescence intensity hardly changes significantly. At pH 7–9, the PL intensity is highest which demonstrated that it is affable to neutral systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of morin
ce:section.ce:para: The temperature dependent PL behavior of the morin/B,N-CDs system were displayed in Fig. S2. The above results illustrate that morin detection based on B,N-CDs appears to be less sensitive at temperatures of 10 °C than at temperature of 30 °C and 60 °C, whereas the sensitivity of 60 °C and 30 °C is similar. So The experiments were carried out at room temperature (about 25 °C). The effect of pH on the fluorescence performance of B,N-CDs in the presence and absence of morin are further illustrated in Fig. S3. It can be found that the B,N-CDs fluorescence intensities are basically stable and decrease uniformly in the pH range of 1–13 with the introduction of morin. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs in the secondary water was also measured with the adding the morin. The experimental results showed that the fluorescence intensities of the B,N-CDs in the secondary water and buffer solution are basically identical. Furthermore, the decrease of the fluorescence intensity is also basically similar after adding morin. So the system for detecting morin was suitable in the secondary water. To evaluate the selectivity of the B,N-CDs, the effects on B,N-CDs fluorescence of 14 kinds of antibiotics and drugs, 12 kinds of metal ions, 14 kinds of anion, 13 kinds of amino acids, 12 kinds of rare earth ions, other similar possible active ingredients were investigated and the records were displayed in Fig. 5 a, Fig. S4a, S4b, S4c, S4d, S4e, respectively. Other substances have no obvious quenching to the fluorescence of B,N-CDs. The selective experiments of Cur, quercetin, rutin, and catechin, which have similar structures to morin. It can be seen from the figure that the reduction in the fluorescence intensity of the B,N-CDs at 467 nm caused by these substances with 10 times the concentration of morin is not as prominent as the morin. Moreover the other four compounds have no fluorescence at 560 nm, therefore they cannot constitute a ratiometric fluorescence detection. Meanwhile, in the existence and absence of morin, some existing antibiotics and drugs of ultrahigh concentrations (0.1 mol/L, 100 μL) do not induce significant fluorescence change (Fig. 5b), which means that the method for determining morin based on B,N-CDs has good anti-interference ability. Meanwhile, the time for the interaction between B,N-CDs and morin was investigated (Fig.S5), indicating that the interaction between the two occurred instantly (25 s). Different concentrations of morin was introduced to B,N-CDs solution, meanwhile the fluorescence intensity was tested. The outcome was exhibited in Fig. 5c. With the increasing of the morin concentration, the fluorescence intensity of 467 nm gradually decreased and an increase at 560 nm. Results for the intensity ratio of the two emission channels (F560 nm/F467 nm) in the range of 0–191 μmol/L were exhibited (Fig. S6), meanwhile a good linearity with morin concentrations ranging from 14.5 to 32.5 μmol/L, and the detection of limit (LOD) was recorded to be 0.3 μmol/L (S/N = 3). The linear relationship are F560 nm/F467 nm = 0.01684Cmorin + 0.1286, R2 = 0.9942, n = 10 (Fig. 5d), where C is the concentration of morin. Table 1 lists the detection of morin by different methods reported in the literature. By comparing the results of detection limit and linear range, it can be seen that the B,N-CDs as a fluorescent probe for detecting morin has simple operation and a lower detection limit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching mechanism of B,N-CDs by morin
ce:section.ce:para: For better exploring the quenching mechanism of morin to B,N-CDs, fluorescence decay curve (Fig. 6 a) was measured and the average lifetimes of B,N-CDs exhibits only slightly from 5.50 ns to 5.65 ns. This reveals that the quenching of B,N-CDs by morin is classified static quenching process. Meanwhile, the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of B,N-CDs, morin and B,N-CDs/morin is exhibited in Fig. 6b. Contrasted with the absorption peaks of morin and B,N-CDs alone, the absorption peak of B,N-CDs/morin has red shifted obviously. Subsequently, the absorption spectrum of the B,N-CDs/morin system in pace with the increasing of morin was discussed. In Fig. 6c, when the concentration of morin changes from 14.5 μmol/L to 32.5 μmol/L, the peaks at 278 nm and 418 nm are the red shift compared with the peaks of morin alone at 255 nm and 359 nm, respectively. All these results indicate that B,N-CDs can interact with morin to form a static complex, which prompts the quenching of fluorescence. In addition, the Zeta potentials of B,N-CDs/morin (Fig.S7a), B,N-CDs (Fig.S7b) and morin (Fig.S7c) were −0.1953, −0.2989 and −0.1176 mV, respectively. And the Zeta potentials of the three are all negative, indicating that the surface of them has a negative charge. The zeta potential tends to morin and is somewhere in between after the B,N-CDs reacted with morin, revealing that there is a certain charge neutralization effect occurs. By comparing the FTIR spectrum of B,N-CDs, morin, B,N-CDs/morin (Fig. S8), it can be seen that the stretching vibration of peaks at 3423 cm−1 of –OH and 1569 cm−1 of –C = O move towards low frequencies, and the peak at 2369 cm−1 (stretching vibration of C≡C, or C≡N, or C=C=C, or C=N=C) disappears which means that the benzene-like ring structure on the surface of the B,N-CDs has changed. In summary, the B element on the surface of B,N-CDs is electron-deficient. When it comes into contact with compounds rich in hydroxyl or carbon groups, and it may be due to the attraction of B atoms to the surface electrons of O atoms to form D+-A- electron pairs, which results in the energy of –OH and -C=O being reduced. So it appears as the neutralization of the zeta potential and the red shift of the corresponding groups in the UV–Vis and FTIR spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of morin in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: So as to further verify the operability of this method for the determination of morin in urine, a spike test was complied. The existence of morin was not detected inside the actual sample and then the sample was subjected to a blank spike recovery test. It can be seen from Table 2 that the concentrations of morin detected in the sample is basically the same as the added concentration. The recovery rate is 99.8% to 104.5% and the relative standard deviation is between 1.9% and 2.8%. All these results manifest that the proposed method may detect the morin in urine.

In short, we introduced a casual inexpensive single-step hydrothermal treatment for the preparation of fluorescent B,N-CDs, which have excellent fluorescence properties. The obtained B,N-CDs performed high-sensitivity to detection morin in view of ratiometric fluorescence varies at 467 nm and 560 nm. The as-prepared B,N-CDs exhibited great linearity from 14.5 to 32.5 μmol/L with LOD of 0.3 μmol/L in the determination of morin. In addition, the as-constructed fluorescent technology to detect morin was successfully applied to urine which the recoveries vary from 99.8% to 104.5% and all RSD were found lower than 2.8%. These results demonstrate that our proposed fluorescence method is applicable.

Yanan Yan: Analyzed the data, Resources, Writing -original draft,Writing- review & editing. Lin Li: Project administration, Supervision, Discussed the results. Huilin Zhang: Formal analysis, Discussed the results. Fangfang Du: Supervision. Yating Meng: Investigation, Discussed the results. Shaomin Shuang: Project administration, Supervision. Ruibing Wang: Project administration, Supervision. Chuan Dong: Project administration, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 91843301) and the Applied Basic Research Project of Shanxi Province, China (No. 201901D111038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119751.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
